## Executive Summary
Lee Harvey Oswald had contacts with Soviet officials between his 1959 defection and the assassination, including a meeting with Vice Counsel Kostikov at the Soviet Embassy in Mexico City in October 1963 [1]. The records also mention Oswald's interactions with Soviet Foreign Ministry personnel in Mexico City, but the extent of these interactions is not fully described [4]. The documents do not provide a comprehensive timeline of Oswald's contacts with Soviet officials, but they do offer some details about his activities in Mexico City.

## Detailed Findings
### Oswald's Defection and Early Contacts
Oswald defected to the Soviet Union in 1959, and the CIA was included in the distribution of reports on his contacts with the American Embassy in Moscow [5]. The Central Intelligence Agency was likely interested in Oswald due to counterintelligence concerns [5].

### Oswald's Contacts in Mexico City
In October 1963, Oswald met with Vice Counsel Kostikov at the Soviet Embassy in Mexico City [1]. Kostikov was a member of the KGB and was believed to be with the KGB department that carried out assassinations and sabotage [1]. The records also mention that Oswald had interactions with Soviet Foreign Ministry personnel in Mexico City, but the extent of these interactions is not fully described [4].

### Timeline of Oswald's Activities
The records do not provide a comprehensive timeline of Oswald's contacts with Soviet officials, but they do offer some details about his activities in Mexico City. On September 27, 1963, Oswald visited the Cuban Consulate three times, requesting an in-transit visa to Cuba with Russia as the final destination [2]. On October 1, 1963, Oswald had a telephonic contact with the Soviet Consulate [3]. The records also mention that Oswald's last contact with the Cuban Embassy was on Friday evening, September 27, 1963 [3].

### Gaps in the Records
The records do not directly describe the nature of Oswald's interactions with Soviet officials, and the extent of these interactions is not fully described [4]. The documents also do not provide information about any other contacts Oswald may have had with Soviet officials between his defection in 1959 and the assassination in 1963.
